questionText: Which is better investment: 12% stock at par with an income tax at the rate of 5 paise per rupee, or $14\dfrac{2}{7}\%$ stock at 120 free from income tax?

steps.explanation: Income from 12% stock at par (₹100 face value at ₹100): gross income = 12% of ₹12000 = ₹1440. Income tax = 5% of ₹1440 = ₹72. Net income = ₹1440 - ₹72 = ₹1368.

Income from $14\frac{2}{7}\%$ stock at 120 (free from tax): number of ₹100 face-value shares = 12000/120 = 100. Income = $\frac{100}{7}\%$ of $100 \times 100 = \frac{100}{7} \times 100 = \text{₹}1428.57$.
Compare: ₹1428.57 > ₹1368, so $14\frac{2}{7}\%$ stock at 120 (free from tax) gives higher net income.

solution.shortcut: Compare net yield per ₹100 invested. For taxed stock: net yield = face rate × (1 − tax rate). For tax-free stock: yield = face rate / market price × 100.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to apply income tax to the 12% stock income before comparing.

modelName: Better Investment — Tax vs Tax-Free Stock

questionText: A invested some money in 10% stock at 96. If B wants to invest in an equally good 12% stock, he must purchase a stock worth of:

steps.explanation: A invests in 10% stock at 96: for an income of ₹10, investment = ₹96.

For B's 12% stock to be equally good, it must yield the same return per rupee invested. For an income of ₹12 (which a 12% stock provides on ₹100 face value), the investment must also give the same yield ratio.
So B must buy the 12% stock at a market price of ₹115.20 per ₹100 face value for it to be equally good.

solution.shortcut: Equally good stocks have the same yield. If 10% stock at 96 has yield = 10/96, then for 12% stock: price = 12 × 96/10 = 115.20.
solution.commonMistake: Incorrectly comparing face rates instead of yields. The market price must be adjusted so that the yield (income/investment) is the same.

modelName: Equally Good Investment — Market Price Finder
